vocabulary.en: to become more severe or controlling
vocabulary.excerpt: That dangerous heat wave tightening its grip on the eastern U.S.
vocabulary.example_cn: 热浪正加剧对美国东部的控制。

vocabulary.en: to rise quickly to a high level
vocabulary.excerpt: Temperatures will soar near 100 degrees
vocabulary.example_cn: 气温将飙升至近100华氏度。

vocabulary.en: extremely harsh or unpleasant
vocabulary.excerpt: a feels like temperature of a brutal 110
vocabulary.example_cn: 体感温度高达110华氏度，非常难受。

vocabulary.en: like a miracle; very surprising
vocabulary.excerpt: they're celebrating a miraculous rescue
vocabulary.example_cn: 他们正在庆祝一次奇迹般的救援。

vocabulary.en: broken stones or bricks from a destroyed building
vocabulary.excerpt: A mall security guard was pulled from the rubble
vocabulary.example_cn: 一名商场保安被从废墟中救出。

vocabulary.en: a small space containing air, especially in a collapsed structure
vocabulary.excerpt: He survived in an air pocket
vocabulary.example_cn: 他在一个气穴中幸存下来。

vocabulary.en: the number of people who have died
vocabulary.excerpt: the death toll continues to rise
vocabulary.example_cn: 死亡人数持续上升。

vocabulary.en: an award given for promoting liberty
vocabulary.excerpt: Pope Leo XIV will accept the Philadelphia Liberty Medal
vocabulary.example_cn: 教皇利奥十四世将接受费城自由勋章。

vocabulary.en: a place where many migrants gather or arrive
vocabulary.excerpt: Leo will visit an Italian island that is an migrant hotspot
vocabulary.example_cn: 利奥将访问一个移民热点意大利岛屿。

vocabulary.en: at the most active or intense stage
vocabulary.excerpt: his campaign is in full swing
vocabulary.example_cn: 他的竞选活动正在全面展开。

vocabulary.en: a lead of ten or more points
vocabulary.excerpt: an early, double digit lead in Georgia's Senate race
vocabulary.example_cn: 在佐治亚州参议院竞选中早期领先两位数。

vocabulary.en: a list of people who have been shortlisted for a position
vocabulary.excerpt: Ossoff's name belongs on the short list
vocabulary.example_cn: 奥索夫的名字应列入候选名单。

vocabulary.en: campaigning, especially giving speeches
vocabulary.excerpt: Everything he does on the stump
vocabulary.example_cn: 他在竞选演说中所做的一切。

vocabulary.en: dishonest or illegal behavior by people in power
vocabulary.excerpt: He very carefully directs back toward the issue of corruption
vocabulary.example_cn: 他非常小心地将话题引回腐败问题。

vocabulary.en: to cause to fall or collapse
vocabulary.excerpt: It may topple the government there
vocabulary.example_cn: 这可能会推翻那里的政府。

vocabulary.en: without a clear shape or form
vocabulary.excerpt: corruption, which is a big amorphous concept
vocabulary.example_cn: 腐败，这是一个模糊的大概念。

vocabulary.en: at the expense of; by exploiting
vocabulary.excerpt: They are doing something on the backs of the working people
vocabulary.example_cn: 他们正在以劳动人民为代价做某事。

vocabulary.en: rising in power or influence
vocabulary.excerpt: the ascendant far left flank
vocabulary.example_cn: 崛起的极左翼。

vocabulary.en: the quality of being attractive or exciting
vocabulary.excerpt: That's part of the allure of this guy
vocabulary.example_cn: 这就是这个人的部分魅力所在。

vocabulary.en: an opportunity to gain a seat or advantage
vocabulary.excerpt: one of the biggest pickup opportunities for Republicans
vocabulary.example_cn: 共和党最大的获得席位机会之一。

vocabulary.en: a moment that spreads rapidly online
vocabulary.excerpt: he is having this viral moment
vocabulary.example_cn: 他正处于病毒式传播的时刻。

vocabulary.en: services provided by an elected official to their constituents
vocabulary.excerpt: people talk about his constituent services in Georgia
vocabulary.example_cn: 人们谈论他在佐治亚州的选民服务。

vocabulary.en: a state where both Democrats and Republicans have strong support
vocabulary.excerpt: Georgia is a purple state at best
vocabulary.example_cn: 佐治亚州充其量是一个摇摆州。

vocabulary.en: a person or thing that is certain to win
vocabulary.excerpt: a shoo-in at this point
vocabulary.example_cn: 目前稳操胜券。

vocabulary.en: to hide unpleasant facts about something
vocabulary.excerpt: to whitewash American history
vocabulary.example_cn: 粉饰美国历史。

vocabulary.en: an implicit agreement among members of a society
vocabulary.excerpt: the social contract is frayed
vocabulary.example_cn: 社会契约已经磨损。

vocabulary.en: worn or strained
vocabulary.excerpt: the social contract is frayed
vocabulary.example_cn: 社会契约已经磨损。

vocabulary.en: a state of neglect that leads to uselessness
vocabulary.excerpt: a kind of delinquency of futility
vocabulary.example_cn: 一种无用的失职。

vocabulary.en: directed against a person rather than an argument
vocabulary.excerpt: the ad hominems
vocabulary.example_cn: 人身攻击。

vocabulary.en: demonstrating aggression and willingness to fight
vocabulary.excerpt: it's so bellicose
vocabulary.example_cn: 如此好战。

vocabulary.en: a situation in which no progress is possible
vocabulary.excerpt: a constant state of impasse
vocabulary.example_cn: 持续的僵局状态。

vocabulary.en: manipulating someone into doubting their own reality
vocabulary.excerpt: part of the problem is the gaslighting
vocabulary.example_cn: 部分问题在于心理操纵。

vocabulary.en: a constitutional clause prohibiting officials from accepting gifts from foreign states
vocabulary.excerpt: the Emoluments Clause
vocabulary.example_cn: 薪酬条款。

vocabulary.en: the quality of being honest about one's knowledge and beliefs
vocabulary.excerpt: the golden rule of American political life ought to be intellectual honesty
vocabulary.example_cn: 美国政治生活的黄金法则应该是知识诚实。

vocabulary.en: the practice of claiming to have moral standards that one does not actually have
vocabulary.excerpt: the hypocrisy of liberty
vocabulary.example_cn: 自由的虚伪。

vocabulary.en: larger than usual or expected
vocabulary.excerpt: an outsized number
vocabulary.example_cn: 一个过大的数字。

vocabulary.en: a mine where tungsten is extracted
vocabulary.excerpt: a good deal on a tungsten mine in Kazakhstan
vocabulary.example_cn: 在哈萨克斯坦的钨矿上的一笔好交易。

vocabulary.en: to manipulate a system for personal gain
vocabulary.excerpt: rig the system in their favor
vocabulary.example_cn: 操纵系统以利于自己。

vocabulary.en: to put in danger
vocabulary.excerpt: endangering the national security
vocabulary.example_cn: 危及国家安全。

vocabulary.en: the action of overseeing something
vocabulary.excerpt: the Oversight Committee
vocabulary.example_cn: 监督委员会。

sentences.en: That dangerous heat wave tightening its grip on the eastern U.S., leaving more than 160 million Americans under extreme heat risk today.
sentences.cn: 危险的热浪正加剧对美国东部的控制，今天有超过1.6亿美国人面临极端高温风险。
sentences.structure: 主句（现在分词短语作伴随状语）+ 结果状语（现在分词短语）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'That dangerous heat wave tightening its grip on the eastern U.S.'，其中'tightening'是现在分词，表示主动进行；'leaving...'是现在分词短语作结果状语，表示热浪导致的结果。

sentences.en: And Energy Secretary Chris Wright directed all data centers in the mid-Atlantic to use their backup power supplies instead of drawing electricity from the public grid that way there's enough power for residential air conditioning.
sentences.cn: 能源部长克里斯·赖特指示中大西洋地区的所有数据中心使用备用电源，而不是从公共电网取电，这样就有足够的电力供居民空调使用。
sentences.structure: 主句（主语+谓语+宾语+宾补）+ 目的状语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为'Energy Secretary Chris Wright directed all data centers...to use...'，其中'to use'是宾语补足语；'that way there's enough power...'是目的状语从句，说明这样做的目的。

sentences.en: Behind the scenes there's growing chatter that if Democrats are looking for their next national leader, Ossoff's name belongs on the short list.
sentences.cn: 幕后有越来越多的议论，如果民主党正在寻找下一位全国领袖，奥索夫的名字应该列入候选名单。
sentences.structure: 主句（there be句型）+ 同位语从句（内含条件状语从句）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'Behind the scenes there's growing chatter'；'that...'是同位语从句，解释'chatter'的内容；该从句中包含一个由'if'引导的条件状语从句。

sentences.en: What he does talk about is not really the big base issues or the national issues that are coming up.
sentences.cn: 他真正谈论的并不是那些大的基本问题或即将出现的国家问题。
sentences.structure: 主语从句（What he does talk about）+ 系动词+ 表语（名词短语+定语从句）
sentences.analysis: 'What he does talk about'是主语从句，其中'does'表示强调；'the big base issues or the national issues'是表语；'that are coming up'是定语从句修饰'issues'。

sentences.en: But also linking that very directly to how it affects people's lives.
sentences.cn: 而且非常直接地将这与它如何影响人们的生活联系起来。
sentences.structure: 现在分词短语（承接上文，作并列谓语）
sentences.analysis: 该句是现在分词短语，与上文'not just talking about corruption'并列，表示'不仅谈论腐败，而且将其与人们的生活联系起来'。'how it affects people's lives'是宾语从句。

sentences.en: He talked about -- when he talks about the ballroom being constructed, he says, what's going on is donors who got tax cuts are writing checks to -- for the ballroom, but those tax cuts paid, Ossoff says, and it's true when you look at the math of it, for the subsidy cuts on Obamacare that made health care prices go up for people.
sentences.cn: 他谈到——当他谈到正在建造的舞厅时，他说，实际情况是，获得减税的捐助者正在为舞厅开支票，但奥索夫说，这些减税支付了，而且当你计算一下，确实如此，用于削减奥巴马医改的补贴，这导致人们的医疗价格上涨。
sentences.structure: 主句（he talked about）+ 时间状语从句+ 直接引语（复合句）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'He talked about'；'when he talks about...'是时间状语从句；直接引语中，'what's going on is...'是主语从句作主语，'donors who got tax cuts are writing checks'是表语从句，'but those tax cuts paid'是并列句，'and it's true...'进一步解释，'for the subsidy cuts...'是原因状语。

sentences.en: There is, for example, in Albania, something going on right now called the flamingo revolution.
sentences.cn: 例如，在阿尔巴尼亚，现在正在发生一场被称为“火烈鸟革命”的事件。
sentences.structure: there be句型+ 现在分词短语+ 过去分词短语
sentences.analysis: 'There is...something'是主句；'going on right now'是现在分词短语作定语修饰'something'；'called the flamingo revolution'是过去分词短语作定语。

sentences.en: And it's because Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump have been trying to buy an island to develop a resort there.
sentences.cn: 这是因为贾里德·库什纳和伊万卡·特朗普一直试图购买一个岛屿，在那里开发一个度假村。
sentences.structure: 主句（It's because...）+ 原因状语从句
sentences.analysis: 'It's because...'是主句，'because'引导原因状语从句；从句中'have been trying'是现在完成进行时，表示持续进行的动作。

sentences.en: All the polling I have seen is that Americans care about corruption, but they really care about it when you talk about how this family or the Trump administration or this billionaire is getting rich and richer and richer either through deals, et cetera, and your average American is paying higher prices.
sentences.cn: 我所看到的所有民调都显示，美国人关心腐败，但他们真正关心的是，当你谈到这个家族、特朗普政府或这个亿万富翁如何通过交易等方式变得越来越富，而普通美国人却在支付更高的价格时。
sentences.structure: 主句（All the polling...is that...）+ 定语从句+ 表语从句（内含时间状语从句和并列句）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'All the polling...is that...'；'I have seen'是定语从句修饰'polling'；'that Americans care...'是表语从句；该从句中包含'when you talk about...'时间状语从句，以及'and your average American is paying...'并列句。

sentences.en: Ossoff has managed to maintain relatively inoffensive so far to both the mainstream liberal majority of the Democratic coalition, as well as the ascendant far left flank.
sentences.cn: 到目前为止，奥索夫成功地保持了相对无害的形象，既对民主党联盟中的主流自由派多数，也对崛起的极左翼。
sentences.structure: 主句（主语+谓语+宾语）+ 状语（to both...as well as...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'Ossoff has managed to maintain relatively inoffensive'；'to both...as well as...'是介词短语作状语，说明对谁无害。

sentences.en: Republicans really had a damaging dynamic where the popular incumbent governor of Georgia endorsed one candidate, the president endorsed somebody else.
sentences.cn: 共和党人确实有一个破坏性的动态：佐治亚州受欢迎的现任州长支持一位候选人，总统支持了另一个人。
sentences.structure: 主句（主语+谓语+宾语）+ 定语从句（where引导）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'Republicans really had a damaging dynamic'；'where...'是定语从句修饰'dynamic'，说明动态的具体内容。

sentences.en: And the way that, you know, he gets this -- he has -- he said to me no on the presidential stuff.
sentences.cn: 而且，你知道，他得到这个的方式——他有——他对我说不参与总统事务。
sentences.structure: 主句（And the way...）+ 定语从句（that引导）+ 插入语+ 直接引语
sentences.analysis: 主句为'And the way...'；'that he gets this'是定语从句修饰'the way'；'you know'是插入语；'he said to me no on the presidential stuff'是直接引语。

sentences.en: We've gone from debates about confederate monuments, to fights over critical race theory and diversity, equity, inclusion, and how history should be taught in schools.
sentences.cn: 我们已经从关于邦联纪念碑的辩论，转向了关于批判性种族理论、多样性、公平、包容以及历史应该如何在学校教授的争论。
sentences.structure: 主句（We've gone from...to...）+ 并列宾语
sentences.analysis: 主句为'We've gone from...to...'；'debates about...'和'fights over...'是并列宾语；'how history should be taught'是宾语从句。

sentences.en: When you look at some of the executive orders, for instance, one of the things they do is try to correct for what they see as too much dogma in politics.
sentences.cn: 当你查看一些行政命令时，例如，他们做的一件事就是试图纠正他们认为政治中过多的教条。
sentences.structure: 时间状语从句+ 主句（主语+系动词+表语）+ 定语从句+ 宾语从句
sentences.analysis: 'When you look at...'是时间状语从句；主句为'one of the things...is try to correct'；'they do'是定语从句修饰'things'；'what they see as...'是宾语从句。

sentences.en: One of them saying, they want to 'instead focus on the greatness of the achievements and progress of the American people, respect of natural features, beauty, abundance, and grandeur of the American landscape.'
sentences.cn: 其中一项说，他们希望“转而关注美国人民成就和进步的伟大，尊重美国自然景观的特征、美丽、丰富和壮丽。”
sentences.structure: 现在分词短语（One of them saying）+ 直接引语（并列动词短语）
sentences.analysis: 'One of them saying'是独立主格结构；直接引语中，'focus on'和'respect'是并列的动词短语。

sentences.en: Now, for the Trump administration, this is their vision of what America should be.
sentences.cn: 现在，对于特朗普政府来说，这是他们对美国应该是什么样子的愿景。
sentences.structure: 主句（this is their vision）+ 介词短语（of what...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'this is their vision'；'of what America should be'是介词短语，其中'what America should be'是宾语从句。

sentences.en: We've had a lot of work, a lot of scholarship that shows us that around America's anniversaries there tends to be a push from administrations to whitewash American history, to focus simply on the great acts, but a very specific great acts by specific great people, predominantly white men.
sentences.cn: 我们有很多研究、很多学术成果向我们表明，在美国周年纪念日前后，政府往往会推动粉饰美国历史，只关注伟大的行为，但非常具体的伟大行为是由特定的伟人，主要是白人男性完成的。
sentences.structure: 主句（We've had...）+ 定语从句（that shows us...）+ 宾语从句（that around...）+ 不定式短语（to whitewash...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'We've had a lot of work, a lot of scholarship'；'that shows us...'是定语从句；'that around America's anniversaries...'是宾语从句；'to whitewash...'和'to focus...'是不定式短语作定语修饰'push'。

sentences.en: Americans want to hear about all of the messy, complicated aspects of American history, of United States history.
sentences.cn: 美国人想听到美国历史中所有混乱、复杂的方面。
sentences.structure: 主句（主语+谓语+宾语）+ 介词短语（of...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'Americans want to hear about all of the messy, complicated aspects'；'of American history, of United States history'是介词短语作定语。

sentences.en: Fifty-one percent said once held true but no longer.
sentences.cn: 51%的人说曾经是真的，但不再如此。
sentences.structure: 主句（主语+谓语）+ 宾语从句（省略that）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'Fifty-one percent said'；'once held true but no longer'是宾语从句，省略了'that'。

sentences.en: Many people have commented on the paralysis as a result of the partizan division, the ad hominems, the viciousness of the discourse.
sentences.cn: 许多人评论了由于党派分歧、人身攻击和话语的恶毒而导致的瘫痪。
sentences.structure: 主句（主语+谓语+宾语）+ 介词短语（as a result of...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'Many people have commented on the paralysis'；'as a result of...'是介词短语作原因状语。

sentences.en: And it's not even that it's so bellicose.
sentences.cn: 而且甚至不是因为它如此好战。
sentences.structure: 主句（It's not even that...）+ 表语从句
sentences.analysis: 主句为'It's not even that...'；'that it's so bellicose'是表语从句。

sentences.en: But I think part of the problem is the gaslighting.
sentences.cn: 但我认为部分问题在于心理操纵。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 宾语从句（省略that）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'I think'；'part of the problem is the gaslighting'是宾语从句，省略了'that'。

sentences.en: And the fact that the United States Supreme Court, during the Trump administration's first go, essentially said, that doesn't exist.
sentences.cn: 而且，美国最高法院在特朗普政府第一任期期间基本上说，那不存在。
sentences.structure: 名词短语（the fact that...）+ 同位语从句（that...said...）
sentences.analysis: 'the fact'是主语；'that the United States Supreme Court...said...'是同位语从句；'that doesn't exist'是宾语从句。

sentences.en: And now we have a president who's flying on a plane, in Air Force One, recently debuted that is a gift from Qatar.
sentences.cn: 现在我们有一位总统，他乘坐的飞机是空军一号，最近首次亮相，是卡塔尔赠送的礼物。
sentences.structure: 主句（we have a president）+ 定语从句（who's flying...）+ 定语从句（that is a gift...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'we have a president'；'who's flying...'是定语从句修饰'president'；'that is a gift...'是定语从句修饰'plane'。

sentences.en: And I remember a few years ago when the 1619 Project came out, we never heard the end of it.
sentences.cn: 我记得几年前，当1619项目出来时，我们没完没了地听到它。
sentences.structure: 主句（I remember）+ 时间状语从句（when...）+ 主句（we never heard...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'I remember'；'when the 1619 Project came out'是时间状语从句；'we never heard the end of it'是主句。

sentences.en: It's Douglass who's the first person to really call out in a really concentrated way this idea of the hypocrisy of liberty, a nation for liberty and justice for all that is intimately and violently rejecting that premise for the bulk of the people.
sentences.cn: 正是道格拉斯第一个以非常集中的方式真正指出这种自由的虚伪观念，一个宣称自由和正义为所有人的国家，却亲密而暴力地拒绝为大多数人提供这一前提。
sentences.structure: 强调句型（It is...who...）+ 不定式短语+ 同位语+ 定语从句
sentences.analysis: 'It is Douglass who...'是强调句型；'to really call out...'是不定式短语作定语；'a nation for liberty and justice for all'是同位语；'that is intimately and violently rejecting...'是定语从句修饰'nation'。

sentences.en: I think part of what created the controversy of reevaluating that was, we have to be true to facts.
sentences.cn: 我认为造成重新评估争议的部分原因是，我们必须忠于事实。
sentences.structure: 主句（I think）+ 宾语从句（part of what...was...）+ 表语从句（we have to be...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'I think'；'part of what created the controversy...was...'是宾语从句，其中'what created the controversy'是主语从句；'we have to be true to facts'是表语从句。

sentences.en: So, if we're talking about the founding of this new nation, birthed from our mother country, England, it was 1776 and the Continental Congress.
sentences.cn: 所以，如果我们谈论这个新国家的建立，它诞生于我们的母国英国，那就是1776年和大陆会议。
sentences.structure: 条件状语从句+ 主句（it was...）
sentences.analysis: 'if we're talking about...'是条件状语从句；'birthed from...'是过去分词短语作定语；主句为'it was 1776 and the Continental Congress'。

sentences.en: When asked about it on CNBC where he spoke, the president says, look, there's nothing to see here.
sentences.cn: 当他在CNBC上被问及此事时，总统说，看，这里没什么可看的。
sentences.structure: 时间状语从句（When asked...）+ 主句（the president says）+ 直接引语
sentences.analysis: 'When asked about it...'是过去分词短语作时间状语，相当于'When he was asked...'；'where he spoke'是定语从句修饰'CNBC'；主句为'the president says'；直接引语为'look, there's nothing to see here'。

sentences.en: I was just asking, did you -- were you in -- know about the crypto ventures?
sentences.cn: 我只是在问，你是否——你是否了解这些加密货币企业？
sentences.structure: 主句（I was just asking）+ 直接引语（一般疑问句）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'I was just asking'；直接引语为' did you -- were you in -- know about the crypto ventures?'，其中包含自我修正。

sentences.en: What most Americans are seeing is prices that they are fighting with and struggling with.
sentences.cn: 大多数美国人看到的是他们正在与之斗争和挣扎的价格。
sentences.structure: 主语从句（What most Americans are seeing）+ 系动词+ 表语（名词+定语从句）
sentences.analysis: 'What most Americans are seeing'是主语从句；'prices'是表语；'that they are fighting with and struggling with'是定语从句修饰'prices'。

sentences.en: The problem here is not so much the ways in which Donald and his family continue to rig the system in their favor, it's the number of people who are willing either to look the other way or to enable it.
sentences.cn: 这里的问题与其说是唐纳德和他的家人继续操纵系统以利于自己的方式，不如说是愿意要么视而不见要么促成此事的人数。
sentences.structure: 主句（The problem is not so much...it's...）+ 定语从句（in which...）+ 定语从句（who are willing...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'The problem is not so much...it's...'；'in which Donald and his family continue to rig...'是定语从句修饰'ways'；'who are willing...'是定语从句修饰'people'。

sentences.en: This is the president of the United States endangering the national security of American citizens because he is willing to take money from any foreign entity, no matter how potentially hostile it might be to American interests.
sentences.cn: 这是美国总统危及美国公民的国家安全，因为他愿意从任何外国实体拿钱，无论它可能对美国利益有多么潜在的敌意。
sentences.structure: 主句（This is the president...endangering...）+ 原因状语从句（because...）+ 让步状语从句（no matter how...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'This is the president...endangering...'，其中'endangering'是现在分词作定语；'because...'是原因状语从句；'no matter how...'是让步状语从句。

sentences.en: It says in that last deal they made, 'plaintiffs hereby release, waive, acquit and forever discharge defendants and are hereby forever barred and precluded from prosecuting or pursuing any claims.'
sentences.cn: 在他们达成的最后一笔交易中写道：“原告特此释放、放弃、宣告无罪并永远免除被告的责任，并特此永远被禁止和阻止提起或追究任何索赔。”
sentences.structure: 主句（It says...）+ 介词短语（in that last deal）+ 定语从句（they made）+ 直接引语
sentences.analysis: 主句为'It says'；'in that last deal'是介词短语；'they made'是定语从句修饰'deal'；直接引语为'plaintiffs hereby release...'。

sentences.en: I don't think anybody who voted for him or voted against him in 2024 is going to be shocked by these developments.
sentences.cn: 我认为任何在2024年投票支持他或反对他的人都不会对这些事态发展感到震惊。
sentences.structure: 主句（I don't think）+ 宾语从句（anybody...is going to be shocked）+ 定语从句（who voted...）
sentences.analysis: 主句为'I don't think'；'anybody...is going to be shocked'是宾语从句；'who voted for him or voted against him'是定语从句修饰'anybody'。

sentences.en: I do think that is something that Trump looks at the balance sheet and sort of says, let's have these guys pay us, rather than us doling out money to the rest of the world.
sentences.cn: 我确实认为，特朗普看着资产负债表，有点说，让这些家伙付钱给我们，而不是我们向世界其他地方掏钱。
sentences.structure: 主句（I do think）+ 宾语从句（that is something）+ 定语从句（that Trump looks...and says...）+ 直接引语
sentences.analysis: 主句为'I do think'；'that is something'是宾语从句；'that Trump looks...and says...'是定语从句修饰'something'；直接引语为'let's have these guys pay us...'。

sentences.en: The difference, while he might say it is not illegal, the difference is that, if this were a Democratic president and there was a Republican Congress, you would have oversight -- the Oversight Committee and all sorts of oversight of his family about their -- the financial dealings within this administration under potential contracts.
sentences.cn: 区别在于，虽然他可能会说这不违法，但区别在于，如果这是一位民主党总统，并且有一个共和党控制的国会，你就会有监督——监督委员会和各种对其家庭在潜在合同下本届政府内金融交易的监督。
sentences.structure: 主句（The difference is that...）+ 让步状语从句（while...）+ 条件状语从句（if...）+ 虚拟语气
sentences.analysis: 主句为'The difference is that...'；'while he might say...'是让步状语从句；'if this were...and there was...'是条件状语从句，使用虚拟语气；'you would have oversight...'是主句的虚拟结果。

topics.title: 2026年佐治亚州参议院选举
topics.content: 2026年佐治亚州参议院选举中，民主党现任参议员乔恩·奥索夫（Jon Ossoff）在民调中领先，可能赢得连任。奥索夫以反腐为竞选主题，避免党内派系斗争，强调腐败如何影响普通民众生活。共和党方面，由于州长和总统支持不同候选人，导致内部分裂，奥索夫因此受益。
topics.keywords: Ossoff, Georgia Senate race, corruption, Democratic primary, Republican infighting

topics.title: 美国250周年国庆与国民自豪感下降
topics.content: 2026年7月4日是美国建国250周年，但盖洛普民调显示，仅33%的美国成年人“极为自豪”成为美国人，为25年来最低。讨论涉及特朗普政府试图通过行政命令恢复爱国自豪感，但效果适得其反。历史学家指出，美国人更希望了解历史的复杂面，而非被粉饰。
topics.keywords: America 250, Gallup poll, national pride, Trump administration, historical narrative

topics.title: 特朗普的加密货币利润与利益冲突
topics.content: 特朗普总统在重返白宫的第一年通过加密货币赚取超过14亿美元，引发争议。他在CNBC采访中称此举合法。批评者指出，这涉及国家安全风险，因为外国实体可能通过交易影响政策。由于IRS被禁止审计特朗普家族财务，资金来源不透明。
topics.keywords: Trump, crypto profits, emoluments clause, conflict of interest, foreign entities

topics.title: 美国极端热浪与能源危机
topics.content: 2026年7月初，美国东部遭遇极端热浪，超过1.6亿人面临高温风险，体感温度高达110华氏度。能源部长指示数据中心使用备用电源，以确保居民空调用电。多个州的国庆游行和烟花表演被取消或推迟。
topics.keywords: heat wave, energy crisis, backup power, air conditioning, parade cancellations

topics.title: 委内瑞拉地震救援
topics.content: 委内瑞拉发生毁灭性地震一周后，一名商场保安在废墟中被救出，他在气穴中幸存。死亡人数已超过2200人。救援行动仍在继续。
topics.keywords: Venezuela earthquake, rescue, air pocket, death toll

topics.title: 教皇利奥十四世与费城自由勋章
topics.content: 教皇利奥十四世（首位美国教皇）将接受费城自由勋章，但不会在7月4日访问美国，而是访问意大利的一个移民热点岛屿。他因在全球促进自由而受表彰。
topics.keywords: Pope Leo XIV, Liberty Medal, Philadelphia, migrant hotspot, July 4

topics.title: 阿尔巴尼亚“火烈鸟革命”
topics.content: 贾里德·库什纳和伊万卡·特朗普试图在阿尔巴尼亚购买岛屿开发度假村，引发大规模抗议，可能推翻政府。此事在美国国内讨论较少，但成为国际新闻。
topics.keywords: Albania, flamingo revolution, Jared Kushner, Ivanka Trump, resort development

topics.title: 美国历史教育争议
topics.content: 围绕美国历史教育的争论持续，从邦联纪念碑到批判性种族理论、DEI（多样性、公平、包容）以及学校如何教授历史。特朗普政府强调1776年建国，而1619项目则聚焦奴隶制。历史学家认为，美国人希望了解历史的复杂性。
topics.keywords: history education, 1619 Project, 1776, critical race theory, DEI

raw_transcript:
Return to Transcripts main page

Polls Show Ossoff Leading in Georgia Senate Race; Pride in America Falls; Trump's Crypto Profits. Aired 6:30-7a ET

Aired July 03, 2026 - 06:30   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: Good morning. I'm Audie Cornish. Thank you for joining me on CNN THIS MORNING.

It's now half past the hour. And here's what's happening right now.

That dangerous heat wave tightening its grip on the eastern U.S., leaving more than 160 million Americans under extreme heat risk today. Temperatures will soar near 100 degrees in major cities, including New York, Philadelphia, Boston, and here in D.C. with a feels like temperature of a brutal 110. That means parades and fireworks have been canceled or postponed from Delaware to New Jersey, New York and Pennsylvania.

And Energy Secretary Chris Wright directed all data centers in the mid-Atlantic to use their backup power supplies instead of drawing electricity from the public grid that way there's enough power for residential air conditioning.

And in Venezuela they're celebrating a miraculous rescue more than a week after those devastating earthquakes. A mall security guard was pulled from the rubble Thursday. Officials say he survived in an air pocket. His rescue comes as the death toll continues to rise. More than 2,200 victims have been found so far.

And today, Pope Leo XIV will accept the Philadelphia Liberty Medal. The pope, who's been honored for promoting liberty around the world, will attend the ceremony virtually. Leo is the first American pope. He will not visit the U.S. at all during its 250th year, despite the president's invitation. Leo will visit an Italian island that is an migrant hotspot on July 4th.

And Senator Jon Ossoff almost didn't run for another Senate term, but now his campaign is in full swing and going viral online. New polling from Fox has Senator Ossoff out front with an early, double digit lead in Georgia's Senate race. Behind the scenes there's growing chatter that if Democrats are looking for their next national leader, Ossoff's name belongs on the short list. And the group chat is back to talk about it. Why? Because Isaac Dovere has been writing about this. You actually went down there to talk to Ossoff.

EDWARD-ISAAC DOVERE, CNN SENIOR REPORTER: Yes.

CORNISH: What is he doing differently than the conversation we've been stuck having here? Democratic socialists on the rise. Moderates upset. Who is he in the spectrum?

DOVERE: Well, you know, I asked him about that and he said, I'm not really interested in getting into Democratic factions. I couldn't get him going on it. We sat for quite a while in Savannah last week, and then his rally in Savannah on Saturday that I attended.

What he does talk about is not really the big base issues or the national issues that are coming up. Everything he does on the stump, and even in the conversation with me, he very carefully directs back toward the issue of corruption. And not just talking about corruption --

DOVERE: And corruption of the Trump presidency. But also linking that very directly to how it affects people's lives.

So, look, he had a line that got a lot of people laughing at the beginning of the rally where he said, oh, the Iranians closed the Strait of Hormuz again and so the -- his quote was, the president threw his toys out of the stroller and didn't pass the housing bill.

But again, that's -- or didn't sign the housing bill. That's making it about how it's relevant to people's lives. He talked about -- when he talks about the ballroom being constructed, he says, what's going on is donors who got tax cuts are writing checks to -- for the ballroom, but those tax cuts paid, Ossoff says, and it's true when you look at the math of it, for the subsidy cuts on Obamacare that made health care prices go up for people.

And he's always talking about things like that, but also highlighting things that many people haven't been even thinking about when it comes to the questions around corruption. There is, for example, in Albania, something going on right now called the flamingo revolution. And it's because Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump have been trying to buy an island to develop a resort there. It is causing such protests in Albania that it may topple the government there. And yet it is not something that is being talked about here.

CORNISH: Well, you know -- exactly. So let me bring it to this side of the table because I think that's what's interesting. Taking something like corruption, which is a big amorphous concept, I think, for a lot of people. Even I, on air, you know, I'm trying to explain, this is how this crypto deal works. This is how this contract works. It's complicated. So, what do you think of using that as a way in to the voter?

XOCHITL HINOJOSA, CNN POLITICAL COMMENTATOR: Yes. All the polling I have seen is that Americans care about corruption, but they really care about it when you talk about how this family or the Trump administration or this billionaire is getting rich and richer and richer either through deals, et cetera, and your average American is paying higher prices.

CORNISH: So, they have to be connected to taking the hit financially.

HINOJOSA: They are taking the hit. They are doing something on the backs of the working people.

HINOJOSA: I will say that it's smart for him to talk about that because we all know that the number one issue is the economy. That's what American voters care about. But when you're looking at Ossoff moving forward and his potential future, the other thing you know about the Democratic Party is, they want a fighter. They don't want someone who's going to talk about love and unity in a primary.

CORNISH: Are you sure? Because let's look at Ossoff's campaign image, the "o" for Ossoff looks suspiciously like, I don't know, an o that feels like one, that reminds one of hope and change.

JESSE ARM, VP OF EXTERNAL AFFAIRS, MANHATTAN INSTITUTE: Three stipes through it.

HINOJOSA: But I will say -- but in the primary, yes, but it --

CORNISH: So, this is what the -- when they go low --

HINOJOSA: That -- I don't think -- I think Democrats have retired that.

CORNISH: No, not so much. Let me let you get a word in.

CORNISH: Are we looking at another Obama? And if you are, is that good or bad news for Republicans in the age of Trump? We're in a way different time now where you fight off a candidate like this with a Romney.

ARM: Ossoff has managed to maintain relatively inoffensive so far --

ARM: To both the mainstream liberal majority of the Democratic coalition, as well as the ascendant far left flank.

CORNISH: And we're going to show the cash on hand while you're speaking.

ARM: Yes. Yes. That works.

Look, I think that Ossoff, ultimately the big challenge for him will be that the far left ascendant flank will continue to demand more and more and more. And as they extract that from him, there will be a price to play (ph) with the more moderate majority of the Democratic Party. Ultimately --

CORNISH: In a Senate race or do you mean for 2028?

ARM: Oh, no, no, no, I think he's going to win the Senate race.

CORNISH: Oh, got it. OK.

ARM: But we're already talking about 2028, and that's part of the --

CORNISH: Yes, I was going to say --

ARM: That's part of the allure of this guy. Republicans really had a damaging dynamic where the popular incumbent governor of Georgia endorsed one candidate. The president endorsed somebody else. They're both coming out of that bruised. You know, Mike Collins is going to be the nominee. This was supposed to be one of the biggest pickup opportunities for Republicans this cycle. And now Ossoff is poised to run away with it because he is having this viral moment. But we'll see if he can sustain it. I don't know that he --

CORNISH: Yes, viral without being all about the internet, which I find interesting as well.

DOVERE: Yes. And, look, he's done some of the things that are like basic stuff. People, Republicans -- from Republicans to people on the far left talk about his constituent services in Georgia. But it is really stunning. Georgia is a purple state at best. It's really like a red state that has elected some Democrats.

DOVERE: And Ossoff is running comfortably there.

Now. I asked him, does he think of himself as being in a strong position? He said, I'm the underdog. But look, you showed that money there. The polling that's there. It is -- it is striking to see.

CORNISH: Yes, the vibe is not underdog.

DOVERE: They're -- the last time a Democrat was elected to a second full term in the Senate from Georgia was Sam Nunn in 1990.

DOVERE: And this race is being thought of by Democrats as basically a shoo-in at this point. We'll see if it remains that way. But it is a real testament to what Ossoff has been able to pull off. And the way that, you know, he gets this -- he has -- he said to me no on the presidential stuff. He -- it comes because he pops up once a month with these speeches and gets people talking.

CORNISH: OK. Well, we love a good, he's running story. So, I'm sure we'll be back.

I want to turn back to America's 250th birthday. The country is, frankly, still arguing over its founding. We've gone from debates about confederate monuments, to fights over critical race theory and diversity, equity, inclusion, and how history should be taught in schools. And now there's this new Gallup poll showing just 33 percent of U.S. adults are extremely proud to be an American, but that is the lowest level recorded on that question in 25 years.

So, what does that tell us about where America is in its own story? So, I'm bringing in Leah Wright, CNN political historian and associate professor of history at Johns Hopkin's University, and Alexander Heffner, host of the PBS show "The Open Mind." He's also the co-author of a documentary, "History of the United States."

So, I wanted to talk to you guys because with this Trump administration, they have made restoring pride in our country actually a kind of guiding light. When you look at some of the executive orders, for instance, one of the things they do is try to correct for what they see as too much dogma in politics. One of them saying, they want to "instead focus on the greatness of the achievements and progress of the American people, respect of natural features, beauty, abundance, and grandeur of the American landscape."

If that's the case, and I want to start with you, Leah, why are we seeing this Gallup poll number and pride actually dip further?

LEAH WRIGHT, CNN POLITICAL ANALYST AND HISTORIAN: Well, I think one of the things to point out here is that the Trump administration's actions are having the exact opposite effect, at least amongst the public, and how the public understands and wants to understand American history and America at 250.

Now, for the Trump administration, this is their vision of what America should be. And that's not unusual. We've had a lot of -- you know, we've had a lot of work, a lot of ,scholarship that shows us that arounds -- that around America's anniversaries there tends to be a push from administrations to whitewash American history, to focus simply on the great acts, but a very specific great acts by specific great people, predominantly white men.

But that's not the mood that Americans are in today. Americans want to hear about all of the messy, complicated aspects of American history, of United States history. They understand just simply by virtue of their experience within the United States. Even the experience today of, you know, of inequality, lack of social mobility.

CORNISH: Yes. But can I jump here on that because --

CORNISH: I do wonder, what you're talking about is this feeling that the social contract is frayed, right? WRIGHT: Right.

CORNISH: That somehow the -- we're not holding up our end of the bargain here as institutions.

I want to come to you, Alex, because I'm looking at this polling where it says, do you think the American dream still holds true? Fifty-one percent said once held true but no longer. Just 15 percent said never held true. So, I don't think I'm looking at a populist that is like, the American dream is always BS. There's plenty of people who seem to have thought, yes, there was a lot of things sort of promised and being here and we don't think we're trending in that direction.

ALEXANDER HEFFNER, HOST, PBS "THE OPEN MIND": Audie, I call this a kind of delinquency of futility, or you could also call it the futility of delinquency in American politics. Many people have commented on the paralysis as a result of the partizan division, the ad hominems, the viciousness of the discourse.

And it's not even that it's so bellicose. It's that it has strung us up in a constant state of impasse that we can't feel the achievement of the present. And I always think it's important to return to those founding documents and to hold them up for both the aspirations and the resentments that we experience and have experienced as a country.

But I think part of the problem is the gaslighting. You know, when you look at something like the Emoluments Clause, earlier you were talking about corruption in the Trump administration, and the fact that the United States Supreme Court, during the Trump administration's first go, essentially said, that doesn't exist. And now we have a president who's flying on a plane, in Air Force One, recently debuted that is a gift from Qatar.

So, there are things that just don't add up in the republic right now.

HEFFNER: And let's just be honest about this. We have to be equal opportunity critics. And the golden rule of American political life ought to be intellectual honesty. So, when people talk about Speaker Pelosi --

CORNISH: Well, let me jump in here then --

CORNISH: Just because you're in the modern politics, and I brought you guys on because it's been a time for historians in the last couple of years. I imagine your group chats are all a mess. And I remember a few years ago when the 1619 Project came out, we never heard the end of it. The backlash, the arguing, what should be taught in schools. As we speak, people are talking about textbooks.

I think the Trump administration, and many supporters have pushed back harder on 1776 as the time.

Very quick to you both, to your mind, what year do you see as historians yourself? Is there a particular era you look at and think, that was the real start of America. It doesn't have to be either of those two numbers, or it can be. And can I go to you, Leah.

WRIGHT: 1852 Frederick Douglass' speech, July 5th speech on the anniversary of the 76th anniversary of the Declaration -- signing of the Declaration of Independence, because it's Douglass who's the first person to really call out in a really concentrated way this idea of the hypocrisy of liberty, a nation for liberty and justice for all that is intimately and violently rejecting that premise for the bulk of the people. So, that becomes the story of the nation. That's the one that we remember.

CORNISH: OK, that's one for you. Every historian has one.

Alexander Heffner, for you, when do you think this country began?

HEFFNER: Yes. 1776, we didn't achieve the blessings of liberty -- life, liberty and happiness for some decades and centuries. But I think that the founding period is indisputable as a matter of fact. I think part of what created the controversy of reevaluating that was, we have to be true to facts. So, if we're talking about the founding of this new nation, birthed from our mother country, England, it was 1776 and the Continental Congress.

CORNISH: OK. Well, here from the desk where we write the rough draft of history, usually, I want to thank you guys for being here. I hope you have a good Independence Day holiday.

WRIGHT: Same to you, Audie.

CORNISH: And we're going to kick off America 250 with Anderson Cooper and Andy Cohen as they count down to midnight for historic ball drop honoring the nation's birthday. "Independence Eve Live with Anderson Cooper and Andy Cohen." Coverage begins tonight at 8 p.m. on CNN or, of course, stream it on the CNN app.

Next, we're going to be talking about the president on defense, why he says he did nothing wrong when it comes to the profits he has made in the crypto industry.

Later on CNN, your holiday travel. We know you're on the road. And we're going to be live from Atlanta's airport. Millions of Americans plan to head out this Fourth.

CORNISH: So, the president, not shying away from the fact that he made more than $2 billion in his first year back in the White House. And this includes more than $1.4 billion in crypto ventures. When asked about it on CNBC where he spoke, the president says, look, there's nothing to see here.

JOE KERNAN, CNBC ANCHOR: In the disclosure this week the amount of money that you and the family made in crypto, it was an outsized number.

I was just asking, did you -- were you in -- know about the crypto ventures?

DONALD TRUMP, PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES: No.

KERNAN: So, that was just something --

TRUMP: I could know about it.

KERNAN: That's what I said.

TRUMP: I mean, there's nothing illegal. There's nothing wrong with it.

CORNISH: Group chat is back.

I wanted to play this because we made such a deal out of it yesterday. And he actually got to speak. And I think, interestingly, with a business outlet, right? These are guys. They're no -- they're not foreign to outsized paydays. What do you make of how he's defending it?

DOVERE: Do most Americans know people who made $1.4 billion, $2 billion over the last 15 months or 16 months? I think that that's really what this comes down to for a lot of people. It's, do most Americans know people whose sons got a good deal on a tungsten mine in Kazakhstan? No. What most Americans are seeing is prices that they are fighting with and struggling with. And that is the real --

CORNISH: Yes. We were playing tape yesterday where Megyn Kelly was like, this is grifty (ph). I don't like what the sons are doing. I don't like -- so there's this world of people who were the Hunter Biden hunters who are now, like, OK, guys, wait a second, this doesn't feel good that we have to defend it.

But I do want to bring up this one thing. Mary Trump, longtime Trump critic, but she was on "AC360" and she brought up this issue, which I had not thought about. (BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

MARY TRUMP, Donald TRUMP'S NEICE: The problem here is not so much the ways in which Donald and his family continue to rig the system in their favor, it's the number of people who are willing either to look the other way or to enable it.

This is the president of the United States endangering the national security of American citizens because he is willing to take money from any foreign entity, no matter how potentially hostile it might be to American interests.

CORNISH: OK. I'd like to fact check that. I can't, because, guess what, the IRS is barred from ever looking at the Trump family financial records in terms of audits. It says in that last deal they made, "plaintiffs hereby release, waive, acquit and forever discharge defendants and are hereby forever barred and precluded from prosecuting or pursuing any claims." We might not actually know the sources of some of this money.

ARM: Yes. I mean most Americans don't know people who have made billions of dollars off of crypto. Most Americans also don't know the president of the United States. But most Americans also are totally willing to accept the fact that Trump is a --

CORNISH: But they're also not running Mideast policy, right? No, no, pause for a second.

CORNISH: If you're in charge of policy, isn't that what's supposed to make it distinct?

ARM: Yes, well, I think, again, most Americans sort of take the effort to be rich, stay rich, get richer with Donald Trump baked into the equation. I don't think anybody who voted for him or voted against him in 2024 is going to be shocked by these developments. This is kind of (INAUDIBLE) response.

DOVERE: I mean he said years ago, long before he ever ran for president, I'm the only person who could run for president and actually make money on it. So, this is part of the equation.

CORNISH: Yes. Well, promises made, promises kept.

ARM: Yes, and he talked about making money from foreign adversaries. I do think that is something that Trump looks at the balance sheet and sort of says, let's have these guys pay us, rather than us doling out money to the rest of the world. And certain populist --

CORNISH: Xochitl, let me let you get a word in here. I love when these men are talking, but just in case. HINAJOSA: Yes. No, I -- Donald Trump ran for president in what it

seems like now for two reasons. One, it was a get out of jail free card because you cannot prosecute a sitting president. Two is to essentially benefit financially off the presidency. The difference, while he might say it is not illegal, the difference is that, if this were a Democratic president and there was a Republican Congress, you would have oversight -- the Oversight Committee and all sorts of oversight of his family about their -- the financial dealings within this administration under potential contracts.

CORNISH: Yes. Well, let me end there because --

ARM: (INAUDIBLE) Congress well have that.

CORNISH: Yes, I was about to say, we're heading to the midterms, so you may get your wish.

Let me do group chats because Alex is new to the group chat. And I need to understand your vibe. So, what are people talking about in your world?

ARM: Yes. Well, I've been reading this new Dave Portnoy book, which is kind of a best seller now.

CORNISH: He talked about maybe running for office, Dave Portnoy.

ARM: You took the words out of my mouth.

CORNISH: Yes, I saw that.

ARM: This is the other thing sort of in my group chat. Actually more so than the book, because as Portnoy says in his book, books are for losers and overrated anyway.

CORNISH: Sure. Sure. Open (INAUDIBLE).

ARM: He's talking about running for mayor of New York City against Zohran Mamdani.

ARM: That was something he made headlines with this week. Curtis Sliwa bit back, I think saw someone running into his lane potentially there. So, you know --

CORNISH: Someone got a beret and they started the starting gun.

ARM: It should be interesting.

CORNISH: Xochitl, what about you?

HINAJOSA: So, my group chat is actually called "shake it off." And it is all about Taylor.

CORNISH: Xochitl -- of course.

HINOJOSA: And it is all about Taylor Swift. We already covered it. But I want to say, people are wondering who is invited. More importantly, who is not invited to the wedding.

Things like the dress, what the decor looks like, all those fun things.

CORNISH: OK. OK. We'll come back to it tomorrow.

DOVERE: I mean I guess pretty -- it's the World Cup. This game tonight with Argentina and Cape Verde and what is going to happen there. Could there be a major upset? Perhaps. Or will it just be like a showcase for Lionel Messi to do all of his stuff?

CORNISH: Love the Cinderella story. Love it. Yes. Baby, just say yes.

I'm Audie Cornish. The headlines are next. Thank you so much for being with us here on CNN.

Polls Show Ossoff Leading in Georgia Senate Race; Pride in America Falls; Trump's Crypto Profits. Aired 6:30-7a ET

Aired July 03, 2026 - 06:30   ET

THIS IS A RUSH TRANSCRIPT. THIS COPY MAY NOT BE IN ITS FINAL FORM AND MAY BE UPDATED.

AUDIE CORNISH, CNN ANCHOR: Good morning. I'm Audie Cornish. Thank you for joining me on CNN THIS MORNING.

It's now half past the hour. And here's what's happening right now.

That dangerous heat wave tightening its grip on the eastern U.S., leaving more than 160 million Americans under extreme heat risk today. Temperatures will soar near 100 degrees in major cities, including New York, Philadelphia, Boston, and here in D.C. with a feels like temperature of a brutal 110. That means parades and fireworks have been canceled or postponed from Delaware to New Jersey, New York and Pennsylvania.

And Energy Secretary Chris Wright directed all data centers in the mid-Atlantic to use their backup power supplies instead of drawing electricity from the public grid that way there's enough power for residential air conditioning.

And in Venezuela they're celebrating a miraculous rescue more than a week after those devastating earthquakes. A mall security guard was pulled from the rubble Thursday. Officials say he survived in an air pocket. His rescue comes as the death toll continues to rise. More than 2,200 victims have been found so far.

And today, Pope Leo XIV will accept the Philadelphia Liberty Medal. The pope, who's been honored for promoting liberty around the world, will attend the ceremony virtually. Leo is the first American pope. He will not visit the U.S. at all during its 250th year, despite the president's invitation. Leo will visit an Italian island that is an migrant hotspot on July 4th.

And Senator Jon Ossoff almost didn't run for another Senate term, but now his campaign is in full swing and going viral online. New polling from Fox has Senator Ossoff out front with an early, double digit lead in Georgia's Senate race. Behind the scenes there's growing chatter that if Democrats are looking for their next national leader, Ossoff's name belongs on the short list. And the group chat is back to talk about it. Why? Because Isaac Dovere has been writing about this. You actually went down there to talk to Ossoff.

EDWARD-ISAAC DOVERE, CNN SENIOR REPORTER: Yes.

CORNISH: What is he doing differently than the conversation we've been stuck having here? Democratic socialists on the rise. Moderates upset. Who is he in the spectrum?

DOVERE: Well, you know, I asked him about that and he said, I'm not really interested in getting into Democratic factions. I couldn't get him going on it. We sat for quite a while in Savannah last week, and then his rally in Savannah on Saturday that I attended.

What he does talk about is not really the big base issues or the national issues that are coming up. Everything he does on the stump, and even in the conversation with me, he very carefully directs back toward the issue of corruption. And not just talking about corruption --

DOVERE: And corruption of the Trump presidency. But also linking that very directly to how it affects people's lives.

So, look, he had a line that got a lot of people laughing at the beginning of the rally where he said, oh, the Iranians closed the Strait of Hormuz again and so the -- his quote was, the president threw his toys out of the stroller and didn't pass the housing bill.

But again, that's -- or didn't sign the housing bill. That's making it about how it's relevant to people's lives. He talked about -- when he talks about the ballroom being constructed, he says, what's going on is donors who got tax cuts are writing checks to -- for the ballroom, but those tax cuts paid, Ossoff says, and it's true when you look at the math of it, for the subsidy cuts on Obamacare that made health care prices go up for people.

And he's always talking about things like that, but also highlighting things that many people haven't been even thinking about when it comes to the questions around corruption. There is, for example, in Albania, something going on right now called the flamingo revolution. And it's because Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump have been trying to buy an island to develop a resort there. It is causing such protests in Albania that it may topple the government there. And yet it is not something that is being talked about here.

CORNISH: Well, you know -- exactly. So let me bring it to this side of the table because I think that's what's interesting. Taking something like corruption, which is a big amorphous concept, I think, for a lot of people. Even I, on air, you know, I'm trying to explain, this is how this crypto deal works. This is how this contract works. It's complicated. So, what do you think of using that as a way in to the voter?

XOCHITL HINOJOSA, CNN POLITICAL COMMENTATOR: Yes. All the polling I have seen is that Americans care about corruption, but they really care about it when you talk about how this family or the Trump administration or this billionaire is getting rich and richer and richer either through deals, et cetera, and your average American is paying higher prices.

CORNISH: So, they have to be connected to taking the hit financially.

HINOJOSA: They are taking the hit. They are doing something on the backs of the working people.

HINOJOSA: I will say that it's smart for him to talk about that because we all know that the number one issue is the economy. That's what American voters care about. But when you're looking at Ossoff moving forward and his potential future, the other thing you know about the Democratic Party is, they want a fighter. They don't want someone who's going to talk about love and unity in a primary.

CORNISH: Are you sure? Because let's look at Ossoff's campaign image, the "o" for Ossoff looks suspiciously like, I don't know, an o that feels like one, that reminds one of hope and change.

JESSE ARM, VP OF EXTERNAL AFFAIRS, MANHATTAN INSTITUTE: Three stipes through it.

HINOJOSA: But I will say -- but in the primary, yes, but it --

CORNISH: So, this is what the -- when they go low --

HINOJOSA: That -- I don't think -- I think Democrats have retired that.

CORNISH: No, not so much. Let me let you get a word in.

CORNISH: Are we looking at another Obama? And if you are, is that good or bad news for Republicans in the age of Trump? We're in a way different time now where you fight off a candidate like this with a Romney.

ARM: Ossoff has managed to maintain relatively inoffensive so far --

ARM: To both the mainstream liberal majority of the Democratic coalition, as well as the ascendant far left flank.

CORNISH: And we're going to show the cash on hand while you're speaking.

ARM: Yes. Yes. That works.

Look, I think that Ossoff, ultimately the big challenge for him will be that the far left ascendant flank will continue to demand more and more and more. And as they extract that from him, there will be a price to play (ph) with the more moderate majority of the Democratic Party. Ultimately --

CORNISH: In a Senate race or do you mean for 2028?

ARM: Oh, no, no, no, I think he's going to win the Senate race.

CORNISH: Oh, got it. OK.

ARM: But we're already talking about 2028, and that's part of the --

CORNISH: Yes, I was going to say --

ARM: That's part of the allure of this guy. Republicans really had a damaging dynamic where the popular incumbent governor of Georgia endorsed one candidate. The president endorsed somebody else. They're both coming out of that bruised. You know, Mike Collins is going to be the nominee. This was supposed to be one of the biggest pickup opportunities for Republicans this cycle. And now Ossoff is poised to run away with it because he is having this viral moment. But we'll see if he can sustain it. I don't know that he --

CORNISH: Yes, viral without being all about the internet, which I find interesting as well.

DOVERE: Yes. And, look, he's done some of the things that are like basic stuff. People, Republicans -- from Republicans to people on the far left talk about his constituent services in Georgia. But it is really stunning. Georgia is a purple state at best. It's really like a red state that has elected some Democrats.

DOVERE: And Ossoff is running comfortably there.

Now. I asked him, does he think of himself as being in a strong position? He said, I'm the underdog. But look, you showed that money there. The polling that's there. It is -- it is striking to see.

CORNISH: Yes, the vibe is not underdog.

DOVERE: They're -- the last time a Democrat was elected to a second full term in the Senate from Georgia was Sam Nunn in 1990.

DOVERE: And this race is being thought of by Democrats as basically a shoo-in at this point. We'll see if it remains that way. But it is a real testament to what Ossoff has been able to pull off. And the way that, you know, he gets this -- he has -- he said to me no on the presidential stuff. He -- it comes because he pops up once a month with these speeches and gets people talking.

CORNISH: OK. Well, we love a good, he's running story. So, I'm sure we'll be back.

I want to turn back to America's 250th birthday. The country is, frankly, still arguing over its founding. We've gone from debates about confederate monuments, to fights over critical race theory and diversity, equity, inclusion, and how history should be taught in schools. And now there's this new Gallup poll showing just 33 percent of U.S. adults are extremely proud to be an American, but that is the lowest level recorded on that question in 25 years.

So, what does that tell us about where America is in its own story? So, I'm bringing in Leah Wright, CNN political historian and associate professor of history at Johns Hopkin's University, and Alexander Heffner, host of the PBS show "The Open Mind." He's also the co-author of a documentary, "History of the United States."

So, I wanted to talk to you guys because with this Trump administration, they have made restoring pride in our country actually a kind of guiding light. When you look at some of the executive orders, for instance, one of the things they do is try to correct for what they see as too much dogma in politics. One of them saying, they want to "instead focus on the greatness of the achievements and progress of the American people, respect of natural features, beauty, abundance, and grandeur of the American landscape."

If that's the case, and I want to start with you, Leah, why are we seeing this Gallup poll number and pride actually dip further?

LEAH WRIGHT, CNN POLITICAL ANALYST AND HISTORIAN: Well, I think one of the things to point out here is that the Trump administration's actions are having the exact opposite effect, at least amongst the public, and how the public understands and wants to understand American history and America at 250.

Now, for the Trump administration, this is their vision of what America should be. And that's not unusual. We've had a lot of -- you know, we've had a lot of work, a lot of ,scholarship that shows us that arounds -- that around America's anniversaries there tends to be a push from administrations to whitewash American history, to focus simply on the great acts, but a very specific great acts by specific great people, predominantly white men.

But that's not the mood that Americans are in today. Americans want to hear about all of the messy, complicated aspects of American history, of United States history. They understand just simply by virtue of their experience within the United States. Even the experience today of, you know, of inequality, lack of social mobility.

CORNISH: Yes. But can I jump here on that because --

CORNISH: I do wonder, what you're talking about is this feeling that the social contract is frayed, right? WRIGHT: Right.

CORNISH: That somehow the -- we're not holding up our end of the bargain here as institutions.

I want to come to you, Alex, because I'm looking at this polling where it says, do you think the American dream still holds true? Fifty-one percent said once held true but no longer. Just 15 percent said never held true. So, I don't think I'm looking at a populist that is like, the American dream is always BS. There's plenty of people who seem to have thought, yes, there was a lot of things sort of promised and being here and we don't think we're trending in that direction.

ALEXANDER HEFFNER, HOST, PBS "THE OPEN MIND": Audie, I call this a kind of delinquency of futility, or you could also call it the futility of delinquency in American politics. Many people have commented on the paralysis as a result of the partizan division, the ad hominems, the viciousness of the discourse.

And it's not even that it's so bellicose. It's that it has strung us up in a constant state of impasse that we can't feel the achievement of the present. And I always think it's important to return to those founding documents and to hold them up for both the aspirations and the resentments that we experience and have experienced as a country.

But I think part of the problem is the gaslighting. You know, when you look at something like the Emoluments Clause, earlier you were talking about corruption in the Trump administration, and the fact that the United States Supreme Court, during the Trump administration's first go, essentially said, that doesn't exist. And now we have a president who's flying on a plane, in Air Force One, recently debuted that is a gift from Qatar.

So, there are things that just don't add up in the republic right now.

HEFFNER: And let's just be honest about this. We have to be equal opportunity critics. And the golden rule of American political life ought to be intellectual honesty. So, when people talk about Speaker Pelosi --

CORNISH: Well, let me jump in here then --

CORNISH: Just because you're in the modern politics, and I brought you guys on because it's been a time for historians in the last couple of years. I imagine your group chats are all a mess. And I remember a few years ago when the 1619 Project came out, we never heard the end of it. The backlash, the arguing, what should be taught in schools. As we speak, people are talking about textbooks.

I think the Trump administration, and many supporters have pushed back harder on 1776 as the time.

Very quick to you both, to your mind, what year do you see as historians yourself? Is there a particular era you look at and think, that was the real start of America. It doesn't have to be either of those two numbers, or it can be. And can I go to you, Leah.

WRIGHT: 1852 Frederick Douglass' speech, July 5th speech on the anniversary of the 76th anniversary of the Declaration -- signing of the Declaration of Independence, because it's Douglass who's the first person to really call out in a really concentrated way this idea of the hypocrisy of liberty, a nation for liberty and justice for all that is intimately and violently rejecting that premise for the bulk of the people. So, that becomes the story of the nation. That's the one that we remember.

CORNISH: OK, that's one for you. Every historian has one.

Alexander Heffner, for you, when do you think this country began?

HEFFNER: Yes. 1776, we didn't achieve the blessings of liberty -- life, liberty and happiness for some decades and centuries. But I think that the founding period is indisputable as a matter of fact. I think part of what created the controversy of reevaluating that was, we have to be true to facts. So, if we're talking about the founding of this new nation, birthed from our mother country, England, it was 1776 and the Continental Congress.

CORNISH: OK. Well, here from the desk where we write the rough draft of history, usually, I want to thank you guys for being here. I hope you have a good Independence Day holiday.

WRIGHT: Same to you, Audie.

CORNISH: And we're going to kick off America 250 with Anderson Cooper and Andy Cohen as they count down to midnight for historic ball drop honoring the nation's birthday. "Independence Eve Live with Anderson Cooper and Andy Cohen." Coverage begins tonight at 8 p.m. on CNN or, of course, stream it on the CNN app.

Next, we're going to be talking about the president on defense, why he says he did nothing wrong when it comes to the profits he has made in the crypto industry.

Later on CNN, your holiday travel. We know you're on the road. And we're going to be live from Atlanta's airport. Millions of Americans plan to head out this Fourth.

CORNISH: So, the president, not shying away from the fact that he made more than $2 billion in his first year back in the White House. And this includes more than $1.4 billion in crypto ventures. When asked about it on CNBC where he spoke, the president says, look, there's nothing to see here.

JOE KERNAN, CNBC ANCHOR: In the disclosure this week the amount of money that you and the family made in crypto, it was an outsized number.

I was just asking, did you -- were you in -- know about the crypto ventures?

DONALD TRUMP, PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES: No.

KERNAN: So, that was just something --

TRUMP: I could know about it.

KERNAN: That's what I said.

TRUMP: I mean, there's nothing illegal. There's nothing wrong with it.

CORNISH: Group chat is back.

I wanted to play this because we made such a deal out of it yesterday. And he actually got to speak. And I think, interestingly, with a business outlet, right? These are guys. They're no -- they're not foreign to outsized paydays. What do you make of how he's defending it?

DOVERE: Do most Americans know people who made $1.4 billion, $2 billion over the last 15 months or 16 months? I think that that's really what this comes down to for a lot of people. It's, do most Americans know people whose sons got a good deal on a tungsten mine in Kazakhstan? No. What most Americans are seeing is prices that they are fighting with and struggling with. And that is the real --

CORNISH: Yes. We were playing tape yesterday where Megyn Kelly was like, this is grifty (ph). I don't like what the sons are doing. I don't like -- so there's this world of people who were the Hunter Biden hunters who are now, like, OK, guys, wait a second, this doesn't feel good that we have to defend it.

But I do want to bring up this one thing. Mary Trump, longtime Trump critic, but she was on "AC360" and she brought up this issue, which I had not thought about. (BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

MARY TRUMP, Donald TRUMP'S NEICE: The problem here is not so much the ways in which Donald and his family continue to rig the system in their favor, it's the number of people who are willing either to look the other way or to enable it.

This is the president of the United States endangering the national security of American citizens because he is willing to take money from any foreign entity, no matter how potentially hostile it might be to American interests.

CORNISH: OK. I'd like to fact check that. I can't, because, guess what, the IRS is barred from ever looking at the Trump family financial records in terms of audits. It says in that last deal they made, "plaintiffs hereby release, waive, acquit and forever discharge defendants and are hereby forever barred and precluded from prosecuting or pursuing any claims." We might not actually know the sources of some of this money.

ARM: Yes. I mean most Americans don't know people who have made billions of dollars off of crypto. Most Americans also don't know the president of the United States. But most Americans also are totally willing to accept the fact that Trump is a --

CORNISH: But they're also not running Mideast policy, right? No, no, pause for a second.

CORNISH: If you're in charge of policy, isn't that what's supposed to make it distinct?

ARM: Yes, well, I think, again, most Americans sort of take the effort to be rich, stay rich, get richer with Donald Trump baked into the equation. I don't think anybody who voted for him or voted against him in 2024 is going to be shocked by these developments. This is kind of (INAUDIBLE) response.

DOVERE: I mean he said years ago, long before he ever ran for president, I'm the only person who could run for president and actually make money on it. So, this is part of the equation.

CORNISH: Yes. Well, promises made, promises kept.

ARM: Yes, and he talked about making money from foreign adversaries. I do think that is something that Trump looks at the balance sheet and sort of says, let's have these guys pay us, rather than us doling out money to the rest of the world. And certain populist --

CORNISH: Xochitl, let me let you get a word in here. I love when these men are talking, but just in case. HINAJOSA: Yes. No, I -- Donald Trump ran for president in what it

seems like now for two reasons. One, it was a get out of jail free card because you cannot prosecute a sitting president. Two is to essentially benefit financially off the presidency. The difference, while he might say it is not illegal, the difference is that, if this were a Democratic president and there was a Republican Congress, you would have oversight -- the Oversight Committee and all sorts of oversight of his family about their -- the financial dealings within this administration under potential contracts.

CORNISH: Yes. Well, let me end there because --

ARM: (INAUDIBLE) Congress well have that.

CORNISH: Yes, I was about to say, we're heading to the midterms, so you may get your wish.

Let me do group chats because Alex is new to the group chat. And I need to understand your vibe. So, what are people talking about in your world?

ARM: Yes. Well, I've been reading this new Dave Portnoy book, which is kind of a best seller now.

CORNISH: He talked about maybe running for office, Dave Portnoy.

ARM: You took the words out of my mouth.

CORNISH: Yes, I saw that.

ARM: This is the other thing sort of in my group chat. Actually more so than the book, because as Portnoy says in his book, books are for losers and overrated anyway.

CORNISH: Sure. Sure. Open (INAUDIBLE).

ARM: He's talking about running for mayor of New York City against Zohran Mamdani.

ARM: That was something he made headlines with this week. Curtis Sliwa bit back, I think saw someone running into his lane potentially there. So, you know --

CORNISH: Someone got a beret and they started the starting gun.

ARM: It should be interesting.

CORNISH: Xochitl, what about you?

HINAJOSA: So, my group chat is actually called "shake it off." And it is all about Taylor.

CORNISH: Xochitl -- of course.

HINOJOSA: And it is all about Taylor Swift. We already covered it. But I want to say, people are wondering who is invited. More importantly, who is not invited to the wedding.

Things like the dress, what the decor looks like, all those fun things.

CORNISH: OK. OK. We'll come back to it tomorrow.

DOVERE: I mean I guess pretty -- it's the World Cup. This game tonight with Argentina and Cape Verde and what is going to happen there. Could there be a major upset? Perhaps. Or will it just be like a showcase for Lionel Messi to do all of his stuff?

CORNISH: Love the Cinderella story. Love it. Yes. Baby, just say yes.

I'm Audie Cornish. The headlines are next. Thank you so much for being with us here on CNN.
